Forecast ahead from a regime-switching (HMM) fit

Source: R/engine-hmm.R
nimixForecast.Rd

Draws from the posterior predictive distribution h steps beyond the end of the series, integrating over both parameter uncertainty (one path per posterior draw) and regime uncertainty (the regime is sampled, not fixed).

Usage

nimixForecast(
  object,
  h = 1L,
  newdata = NULL,
  lags = NULL,
  draws = 500L,
  level = 0.9
)

Arguments

object

A FitResult fitted with method = "hmm".

h

Integer horizon (>= 1).

newdata

For a Markov-switching regression, a data frame of h future rows supplying the exogenous predictors. Ignored otherwise.

lags

Named integer vector marking predictors that are the response in disguise, e.g. lags = c(ylag = 1) for a column holding \(y_{t-1}\). Those columns are generated by the forecast, not supplied to it: each posterior draw feeds its own trajectory back as its own lag, so the uncertainty compounds the way an autoregression's should. Give a predictor in lags or in newdata, never both.

draws

Maximum posterior draws to use (thinned evenly).

level

Central credible level for the interval.

Value

A list with summary (a data frame of h rows: mean, median, lower, upper), regime (an h x K matrix of regime probabilities), and draws (the raw draws x h predictive sample).

Details

The recipe is the forward algorithm run one step further: filter the regime distribution to the last observation, push it through the transition matrix \(h\) times, sample a regime from it, and draw from that regime's emission.

What an HMM can and cannot forecast

The point forecast decays toward the stationary mixture as h grows, at rate \(|\lambda_2|^h\) in the second eigenvalue of the transition matrix. That is the model working, not failing: an HMM forecasts the regime, and once the regime is unknowable the best guess for y is the long-run average over regimes. Measured on a two-regime series at +/-2 with persistence 0.95/0.90, the median forecast fell from 1.8 at h = 1 to -1.1 by h = 12, while the regime probabilities went from 0.11/0.89 to 0.59/0.41 – converging on the stationary 0.67/0.33. Reversion, not signal. Interval coverage over the same horizon was 0.917 against a nominal 0.90.

Everything hinges on whether the regime persists. On a Markov-switching regression whose regime held across the forecast boundary, the median forecast beat a constant-mean benchmark fivefold (RMSE 0.50 against 2.65); on a series that happened to switch at exactly the first forecast step – a 5% event under the fitted persistence – it lost (4.42 against 2.45). That is not a defect to tune away: a switch one step ahead is unpredictable by construction, and the model correctly assigned it low probability beforehand. The interval still covered.

Beware the point summary when regimes disagree. With well separated regimes the predictive is bimodal, and its median sits in the trough between the modes – where the model puts almost no mass. $summary is convenient, not faithful; $draws is the honest object, and $regime usually answers the question actually being asked ("which regime will we be in"), degrading far more gracefully than any forecast of y.

For a trend or a seasonal pattern this is the wrong model; nimix does not fit those.

Autoregression

A Markov-switching autoregression is an ordinary Markov-switching regression with the response lagged into the design matrix (nimixReg(y ~ ylag, df, K = 2, method = "hmm")), and forecasting one needs lags rather than newdata: nobody knows the future \(y\), so the lag has to come from the forecast itself. Each posterior draw carries its own trajectory and feeds it back as its own lag, which is what makes the interval widen properly – measured on an MS-AR(1), from 2.15 at h = 1 to 5.12 at h = 10, with RMSE 0.54 against 3.97 for a constant-mean benchmark. With exogenous predictors as well, pass those in newdata and the lags in lags (measured on an MS-ARX: RMSE 0.83 against 5.29).

Do not fake a lag through newdata. It will be taken as a known covariate, held fixed, and the forecast will inherit whatever you invented – confidently and without complaint. lags exists so that you do not have to.

See also

viterbiPath

Examples

if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{
f  <- nimixClust(y, K = 2, method = "hmm")
fc <- nimixForecast(f, h = 6)
fc$summary
fc$regime          # usually the more informative half

# a Markov-switching AR(1): the lag is generated, not supplied
g  <- nimixReg(y ~ ylag, df, K = 2, method = "hmm")
nimixForecast(g, h = 10, lags = c(ylag = 1))
} # }
